title: Market Structures, Profit Maximisation and Game Theory
summary: Four market structures, profit maximisation rule (MR=MC), monopoly vs perfect competition, game theory, prisoner's dilemma, externalities, and ESG.
content_html:
Market Structures, Profit Maximisation and Game Theory

Market structure significantly affects how firms compete and how prices are set.

Four Market Structures

StructureFirmsProductEntry
Perfect CompetitionManyIdenticalEasy
Monopolistic CompetitionManyDifferentiatedEasy
OligopolyFewIdentical or differentiatedDifficult
MonopolyOneUniqueBlocked

Profit Maximisation Rule: MR = MC

All firms, regardless of market structure, maximise profit by producing the output where Marginal Revenue (MR) equals Marginal Cost (MC).

  • If MR > MC: produce more (each additional unit adds to profit).
  • If MR < MC: produce less (each additional unit reduces profit).
  • Economic profit = (P - ATC) x Q

Perfect Competition vs Monopoly

Perfect competition: Firms are price takers (cannot affect market price). MR = P. In the long run, economic profits are zero (entry/exit until normal profit).

Monopoly: Single seller with unique product and blocked entry. Price maker. Earns economic profits even in the long run. MR < P (must lower price to sell more units).

Oligopoly and Game Theory

Oligopoly markets have few sellers who are interdependent -- each firm's decisions affect others. Game theory studies how people behave in strategic situations.

Prisoners' Dilemma: Two players pursuing their dominant strategies both end up worse off than if they had cooperated. Illustrates why cooperation is difficult even when mutually beneficial.

  • Dominant strategy: The best strategy regardless of what the other player does.
  • Nash equilibrium: A situation where each player chooses the best strategy given the strategies of others. Neither player wants to change unilaterally.

Externalities

  • Externality: A benefit or cost affecting someone not directly involved in production or consumption.
  • Positive externality: Benefits others without payment (e.g. education, vaccination).
  • Negative externality: Imposes costs on others without compensation (e.g. pollution).
  • Government remedies: Tax goods with negative externalities; subsidise goods with positive externalities.

exercises.title: Profit maximisation rule
exercises.prompt: All firms maximise profit by producing output where:
options: Total Revenue equals Total Cost
Average Revenue equals Average Cost
Marginal Revenue equals Marginal Cost
Price equals Average Fixed Cost

exercises.title: Perfect competition entry
exercises.prompt: In perfect competition, entry into the industry is:
options: Blocked completely
Difficult due to economies of scale
Easy, leading to zero economic profits in the long run
Regulated by government licences
exercises.answer: Easy, leading to zero economic profits in the long run

exercises.title: Monopoly long run
exercises.prompt: A monopolist can earn economic profits in the long run because:
options: They produce at minimum average cost
Entry into the market is blocked, preventing competition
They operate in perfect competition
Marginal revenue exceeds price
exercises.answer: Entry into the market is blocked, preventing competition

exercises.title: Dominant strategy
exercises.prompt: A dominant strategy in game theory is:
options: The strategy that only works if the other player cooperates
The best strategy for a firm regardless of what other firms do
A strategy requiring perfect information
The strategy that maximises industry profits
exercises.answer: The best strategy for a firm regardless of what other firms do

exercises.title: Nash equilibrium
exercises.prompt: A Nash equilibrium is a situation where:
options: All players cooperate to maximise joint profit
Each player chooses the best strategy given the strategies of others -- no one wants to change unilaterally
One player dominates all others
The government sets prices for all firms
exercises.answer: Each player chooses the best strategy given the strategies of others -- no one wants to change unilaterally

exercises.title: Negative externality remedy
exercises.prompt: To correct a negative externality (e.g. pollution), the government should:
options: Subsidise the good
Tax the good to internalise the external cost
Ban the good entirely
Nationalise the industry
exercises.answer: Tax the good to internalise the external cost

exercises.title: Oligopoly characteristic
exercises.prompt: The key feature of oligopoly is:
options: Many competing firms selling identical products
A single seller with no close substitutes
Interdependence -- few firms where each firm's decisions affect others
Free and easy entry with differentiated products
exercises.answer: Interdependence -- few firms where each firm's decisions affect others

exercises.title: Positive externality
exercises.prompt: An example of a positive externality is:
options: A factory polluting a nearby river
The noise from a busy road
The benefits to society from someone receiving a university education
Congestion caused by driving
exercises.answer: The benefits to society from someone receiving a university education
